mission.title: Install and Configure Linkerd on Kubernetes
mission.description: Linkerd is an ultralight, security-first service mesh for Kubernetes that enhances observability, reliability, and security for your microservices without requiring code changes. Installing Linkerd allows you to manage service-to-service communication in a secure and efficient manner.

steps.title: Add the Linkerd Helm repository
steps.description:
First, you need to add the Linkerd Helm repository to your local Helm setup. Run the following command:
```bash
helm repo add linkerd https://linkerd.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Linkerd control plane
steps.description:
Now, install the Linkerd control plane using Helm. Make sure to create a namespace for Linkerd:
```bash
helm install linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --create-namespace --version edge-26.2.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
To verify that the Linkerd control plane is running, check the status of the pods:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace linkerd
```

steps.title: Check Linkerd dashboard
steps.description:
You can access the Linkerd dashboard to visualize your service mesh. Run the following command to open the dashboard:
```bash
linkerd dashboard --namespace linkerd
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance and reliability, you may want to configure resource limits for the Linkerd components. Edit the `values.yaml` file and set appropriate resource requests and limits for the control plane components. Then, upgrade the release:
```bash
helm upgrade linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --values values.yaml
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Linkerd will show all control plane pods running in the 'linkerd' namespace when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace linkerd`. You can also access the Linkerd dashboard to manage and visualize your service mesh.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add linkerd https://linkerd.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --create-namespace --version edge-26.2.1
helm upgrade linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --values values.yaml

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Linkerd control plane, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd --all
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the Linkerd namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace linkerd
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of the Linkerd control plane. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace linkerd -o yaml > linkerd-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then upgrade the Linkerd control plane to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade linkerd-control-plane linkerd/linkerd-control-plane --namespace linkerd --version edge-26.2.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the Linkerd control plane is running with the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace linkerd
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see pods in the CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more details:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace linkerd
``` 
Look for any error messages that might indicate configuration issues or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: Linkerd dashboard not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If the Linkerd dashboard is not accessible, ensure that the dashboard service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace linkerd
``` 
If the service is not running, check the logs of the dashboard pod for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Control plane components not running
troubleshooting.description:
If any control plane components are not running, check their status and logs:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace linkerd
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace linkerd
``` 
Investigate any errors in the logs to identify the root cause.

troubleshooting.title: Issues with service communication
troubleshooting.description:
If you experience issues with service communication, check the Linkerd proxy logs for the affected services:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -c linkerd-proxy --namespace <service-namespace>
``` 
Look for any indications of misconfiguration or network issues.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
